name: Ensure IAM Users that are inactive for 30 days or more are deactivated
body:
### Security objective

This control ensures that **stale AWS IAM users do not remain as standing access**.

### Assessment detail

The check identifies IAM users that have been inactive for 30 days or more while still existing as access-bearing identities in the account.

### Security impact

Inactive IAM users are often legacy automation accounts, departed-user access, temporary migration users, or forgotten break-glass paths. They may still have passwords, access keys, group memberships, and attached policies. If those credentials exist in old scripts, laptops, tickets, or repositories, they remain useful to an attacker.

The preferred state is federated access through IAM Identity Center or another identity provider. Any exceptional IAM users should have a clear owner, MFA, minimal permissions, and periodic review.
description: AWS IAM users that have been inactive for 30 days or more.
remediationDescription:
## Remediation

### AWS

Deactivate or remove IAM users that have been inactive for 30 days or more. This control identifies IAM users returned by the inactive-user query, so the user must either be removed, deactivated, or brought under a documented access pattern that no longer appears as stale active access.

1. Review the affected IAM user and confirm whether it is still required.
2. If the user is not required, delete access keys, remove the console password, detach policies, remove group memberships, and delete the user.
3. If the user is a break-glass or temporary user, rotate credentials, enforce MFA, and document an exception with an expiry date.
4. Prefer IAM Identity Center or federated roles instead of long-lived IAM users.
5. Re-run sync and assessment after cleanup.

Review the user:

~~~bash
aws iam get-user --user-name {{asset.name}}
aws iam list-access-keys --user-name {{asset.name}}
aws iam list-groups-for-user --user-name {{asset.name}}
aws iam list-attached-user-policies --user-name {{asset.name}}
~~~

Disable access:

~~~bash
aws iam delete-login-profile --user-name {{asset.name}}
aws iam update-access-key \
  --user-name {{asset.name}} \
  --access-key-id <access-key-id> \
  --status Inactive
~~~

Delete the user after dependencies are removed:

~~~bash
aws iam delete-user --user-name {{asset.name}}
~~~

References:
- https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_users_manage.html
- https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_credentials_access-keys.html
- https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/latest/userguide/what-is.html

The control is satisfied when inactive IAM users no longer have active console or access-key credentials, or when the user is removed.
